coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of Si-doped carbon quantum dots with up-conversion fluorescence as fluorescent probes for dual-readout detection of berberine hydrochloride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Jia-Man
Zhang, Fang
Zhu, Yu-Lin
Deng, Xiao-Yan
Chen, Xian-Ping
Zhou, Qiu-Ju
Tan, Ke-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Here, the high fluorescent silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) were prepared by a facile and one-pot hydrothermal assay using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane as the carbon and silicon source. The prepared Si-CQDs exhibit favorable water-soluble, high-temperature resistance, acid resistance, alkali resistance, high ionic strength resistance, high photostability, film-forming ability and solid-state fluorescence. Compared to other Si-CQDs that have been reported, the prepared Si-CQDs show unique up-conversion fluorescence. Furthermore, it is found that berberine hydrochloride (BH) can effectively quench the down- and up-conversion fluorescence of the Si-CQDs, making it can be used as a highly sensitive and specific probe for BH dual-mode sensing. Meanwhile, the linear range of down-conversion fluorescence detection for BH is 0.5-30.0 µmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 50 nmol/L, and the linear range of up-conversion fluorescence assay for BH is 0-25.0 µmol/L. The mechanism of down-conversion fluorescence quenching by BH was investigated through a series of studies. The results show the quenching mechanism is the inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, this proposed strategy has been well used to analyze BH in urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon-doped Carbon Quantum Dots
Fluorescence
Up-conversion
Berberine Hydrochloride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability and film-forming ability of the Si-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the Si-CQDs. (a) TEM image, inset: the high-resolution TEM image; (b) Particle size distribution; (c) Hydrodynamic size distribution of the Si-CQDs.
(a) The FTIR spectrum; (b) XPS survey spectrum of the Si-CQDs. The XPS patterns of (c) C 1s; (d) O 1s; (e) N 1s and (f) Si 2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. Inset: photographs are taken under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the ATMOS and Si-CQDs; (c) The PL spectra of the Si-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (d) Up-conversion PL properties of the Si-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the Si-CQDs without and with different contents of BH; (b) ΔF under different contents of BH, inset: the calibration curve; (c) Up-conversion fluorescence spectra of the Si-CQDs with different content of BH, inset: the calibration curve of up-conversion fluorescence assay; (d) The PL spectra of the Si-CQDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of BH.
(a) The fluorescence lifetime of Si-CQDs and Si-CQDs in the presence of 20 μmol/L BH; (b) The UV absorption spectra of BH, Si-CQDs and Si-CQDs with 20 μmol/L BH.
Schematic illustration of synthesis of Si-CQDs and the detection of BH.
The comparison of various methods for BH detection.
Results of the detection of BH in urine samples using the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation for five measurements (n = 5).

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of Si-doped carbon quantum dots with up-conversion fluorescence as fluorescent probes for dual-readout detection of berberine hydrochloride

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. Schematic illustration of synthesis of Si-CQDs and the detection of BH.

Here, the high fluorescent silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) were prepared by a facile and one-pot hydrothermal assay using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane as the carbon and silicon source. The prepared Si-CQDs exhibit favorable water-soluble, high-temperature resistance, acid resistance, alkali resistance, high ionic strength resistance, high photostability, film-forming ability and solid-state fluorescence. Compared to other Si-CQDs that have been reported, the prepared Si-CQDs show unique up-conversion fluorescence. Furthermore, it is found that berberine hydrochloride (BH) can effectively quench the down- and up-conversion fluorescence of the Si-CQDs, making it can be used as a highly sensitive and specific probe for BH dual-mode sensing. Meanwhile, the linear range of down-conversion fluorescence detection for BH is 0.5-30.0 µmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 50 nmol/L, and the linear range of up-conversion fluorescence assay for BH is 0-25.0 µmol/L. The mechanism of down-conversion fluorescence quenching by BH was investigated through a series of studies. The results show the quenching mechanism is the inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, this proposed strategy has been well used to analyze BH in urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: For many years, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have been extensively researched and applied in many fields such as catalysis, electronics, biosensing and bioimaging for their tunable luminescence properties. Whereas heavy metals are the essential elements of semiconductors, QDs have prompted potential health and environmental issues [1,2]. Differ from traditional semiconductor QDs, carbon-based nanomaterial, mainly composed of non-toxic carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms, have drawn increasing attention because of their superior physicochemical properties compared with semiconductor QDs [3]. Among all carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as the carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials that comprise discrete carbogenic nanoparticles with diameter below 10 nm, which were accidentally discovered during the electrophoretic purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes prepared by arc-discharge method, have recently attracted intensive attention [4,5].

As promising fluorescent materials, CQDs have a series of merits, including low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, tunable photoluminescence (PL) spectra, high photostability, high catalytic activity. etc [6-13]. Owing to their fascinating advantages, CQDs’ synthetic methods have made great progress. Many preparation approaches have been developed, such as microwave method [14], electrochemical synthesis [15], thermal decomposition [16], chemical oxidation [17], ultrasonic method [18], hydrothermal method [19] and so on. Among them, hydrothermal method is regarded as a facile and efficient strategy for the preparation of CQDs. However, most obtain-CQDs without passivation have weak fluorescence and low quantum yields (QY) [20], limiting CQDs’ applications in chemistry, biology, and life sciences. To improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs, surface functionalization and heteroatoms doping are two of the most effective approaches [21]. Some heteroatoms doping of CQDs such as nitrogen [22-28], sulphur [27,29], boron [30,31], phosphorus [32], germanium [33] and selenium [34,35] have been reported. Similar to germanium and selenium, as one of group IV, Si has good biocompatible and environment-friendly, which provides a promising direction for doping of CQDs. Lin [36], Qian [37] and Gao et al. [38] synthesized Si-doped CQDs by carbonizing carbon source in the presence of extra silicon source. Liu et al. [16,39] synthesized a series of organosilane-functionalized carbon dots by pyrolyzing organic polybasic acid in silanes. Talited et al. [40] synthesized organosilane-functionalized carbon nanodots by pyrolyzing citric acid in a silane coupling agent. Except, few studies about Si-CQDs were reported. Therefore, it was desirable to synthesize Si-CQDs for further investigations and applications.

Berberine hydrochloride (BH), a critical anti-inflammatory drug for heart and intestinal disorders, possesses anti-tumor promotion and anti-lipase properties [41]. In clinic medical analysis and drug testing, it is meaningful to the quantitative determination of BH. Currently, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is the major method used to efficiently detect BH [42]. Although HPLC analysis has high selectivity and sensitivity, it requires extensive sample preparation, time-consuming and expensive instruments. Thus, it is essential to develop a facile, reliable and low-cost analytical strategy for the sensing of BH.

This work developed a simple route for preparing excellent PL performance Si-CQDs using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane as the carbon and silicon sources (Scheme 1 ). Owe to the superiorities of organosilicone, the Si-CQDs show excellent high temperature resistance, acid resistance, alkali resistance, high ionic strength resistance, high photostability, good film-forming ability and solid-state fluorescence. Compared with other reported Si-CQDs or organosilane-functionalized carbon dots, the prepared Si-CQDs show specific up-conversion fluorescence [16,36-40], which is particularly attractive for in vivo bioimaging due to the good photon tissue penetration and low background auto-fluorescence. And the prepared Si-CQDs have been used as a dual-readout assay for high sensitivity and selectivity determination of BH. The LOD is as low as 50 nmol/L. Furthermore, the established assay has been utilized for the sensing of BH in urine samples.

(3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMOS, 97%) and berberine hydrochloride (BH, purity > 98%, HPLC) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents used in the experiment were analytical grade, and ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ.cm) produced by a Milli-Q ultrapure water purifier (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments. Britton-Robinson buffer solution (BR) was used to control the acidity of the aqueous solutions.

All absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) was used to characterize the morphology of the Si-CQDs. Elemental and functional groups analyses were made on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and FT-IR 8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tyoto, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime was measured with an FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin YvonInc., France). The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential were obtained after suitable dilution of Si-CQDs solution at 25℃, by dynamic laser light scattering (ZLS) (ZEN3690, Malvern).

Si-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Typically, 850 µL 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane and 3150 µL sulfuric acid (0.05 mol/L) were added into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 9 h, the solution changed from colorless to pale yellow during this process, indicating the formation of the Si-CQDs. Afterwards, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The pale yellow transparent liquid was obtained. Next, the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min to dislodge the non-fluorescent deposit and got the upper Si-CQDs aqueous solution for further use. Then, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7, and then the solution was subjected to dialysis against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 24 h to remove small molecules. Finally, Si-CQDs were concentrated by freeze −80 °C and dried under vacuum.

The QY of the Si-CQDs was obtained by comparing the integrated PL intensities and the absorbance values of the Si-CQDs using quinine sulfate (QYstd = 0.54) as the standard and calculated with the following equation [43]:
Q
Y CDs
= Q
Y std
(
I CDs
/
I std
)
(
A std
/
A CDs
)
(
η
CDs
2
/
η
std
2
)

Where A is the absorbance, I refers to the integrated PL intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent. And the Si-CQDs were dissolved in water (refractive index η = 1.33) and quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (η = 1.33). In order to minimize the reabsorption effects, the absorbance value of the individual solution was kept below 0.08 at the 370 nm excitation wavelength.

20.0 μL Si-CQDs solution (0.27 mg/mL) and 100 μL BR buffer solution (pH 7.24) were added into a 1.5 mL microtube. Next, different concentrations of BH were added, and diluted to 1.0 mL with ultra-pure water. Then the mixture was fully vortexed after each addition of the additives. After equilibrating for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured.

To get the highest fluorescence efficiency of the Si-CQDs, several factors, such as dosage of APTMOS, reaction time, reaction temperature, were investigated. Single-factor-experiment results (Fig. S1a and Fig. S1b) indicate the obtained Si-CQDs have the stronger fluorescence when the volume of APTMOS is 800 µL and reaction time is 9 h. Orthogonal experiment results show (Table S1) the synthesized Si-CQDs have the strongest fluorescence under the condition that the reaction temperature is 190 °C, the reaction time is 9 h as well as the carbon source (APTMOS) dosage of 850 µL. Considering that reaction solvent and acidity will influence the hydrolyze, condensation polymerization and carbonization of the hydrothermal reaction process. So, different reaction mediums and acidity of the reaction system were studied. Table S2 shows that 0.05 mol/L H2SO4 water medium is the optimum condition.

The size and morphology of obtained Si-CQDs can be observed by TEM and DLS. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM images of the Si-CQDs, and Si-CQDs are mostly spherical dots that are uniformly dispersed. The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig.la) displays well-resolved lattices fringes with lattice spacings of 0.24 nm, which correspond to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [31,44]. Moreover, the Si-CQDs show a narrow distribution range of 1.05-3.05 nm with an average size of about 2.12 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment of the Si-CQDs was also measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. S2. The DLS data show a narrow hydrodynamic size distribution range of 1.4-4.9 nm, which is consistent with TEM results.

The surface functionalities were analyzed by the FT-IR and XPS spectra. Fig. 2 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the Si-CQDs. The broad band centered at 3425.58 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH [17], and the peak located at 1627.29 cm−1 is contributed to the COO of the Si-CQDs [45], which indicates that carboxylate and hydroxyl groups exist on the surface of the Si-CQDs. The characteristic peak at 3047.53 cm−1 is from the stretching mode of NH groups, and the peak at 1508.33 cm−1 is assigned to NH bonds [44]. The peak about 2924 cm−1 is due to C-H vibrational modes with symmetric CH2 of the Si-CQDs [14]. The 856.4 cm−1 and 1118.71 cm−1 belong to SiOSi and SiOC peaks, revealing the formation of siloxane groups by hydrolyzation during the heating process [37]. The peak at 1041.56 cm−1 can be attributed to CO bonds [46]. XPS spectra were also performed to study the composition of the Si-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the XPS spectrum indicated that the Si-CQDs are mainly composed of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), silicon (Si) and sulfur (S) elements, and the relative percentage composition are 54.5%, 28.06%, 7.23%, 6.92% and 3.3%, respectively, which reveals the core of the Si-CQDs are mainly constructed by carbon. The C1s XPS spectra of Si-CQDs can be decomposed into four contributions with binding energies at about 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.7 eV (Fig. 2c). The main contribution at low binding energy (284.6 eV) can be attributed to the sp2 hybridized carbon in aromatic rings, suggesting the aromatization of APTMOS during the heating process, and the peak at 285.4 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.7 eV can be attributed to CN, CO and CO bands, respectively [47,48]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed the oxygen signals of CO at 531.5 eV and CO at 532.3 eV [25]. As shown in Fig. 2e, there are two peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.3 eV in the N1s spectrum, which belong to the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [23]. The Si 2p spectrum can be divided into two bonds, namely SiO at 101.9 eV, and Si-N at 102.6 eV(Fig. 2f) [37]. The above results reveal that the synthesized Si-CQDs are capped with abundant amino and hydroxyl groups, which greatly improves the aqueous solubility and stability of the Si-CQDs, and is convenient for surface modification and functionalization. The zeta potential of the Si-CQDs was also determined to be slightly negative, around −6 mV (Fig. S3). These results indicate that a large amount of negatively charged moieties (such as COOH, OH) and a few positively charged moieties (such as NH3 +) exist on the surface of the Si-CQDs.

To investigate the spectral properties of the Si-CQDs, the absorption, excitation and emission spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The absorption spectrum of the Si-CQDs exhibits that the maximum absorption peak is located at 270 nm, representing the typical absorption of π → π* transition [49]. While there is no absorption peak at 270 nm of the APTMOS without heating, indicating that the honeycomb frame is obtained through the hydrothermal procedure (Fig. 3b). The inset photograph of the Si-CQDs solution is transparent and clear under daylight and exhibited strong blue emission under 365 nm UV light. The maximum emission wavelength is at 452 nm when excited at 370 nm wavelength. And the fluorescence spectra were measured at 310-380 nm excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3c, like most CQDs, the fluorescence emissions of the Si-CQDs depends on their excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peaks shift to longer wavelengths. This behavior is closely related to different sizes or different surface states distribution of the Si-CQDs. The property of excitation-dependent PL emission is easy to construct the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. We can readily control the emission wavelength of the donor (Si-CQDs) and allow for well energy matching between the donor and the acceptor.

Meanwhile, the Si-CQDs have a high QY of 34.8% compared with the reference of 0.1 mol/L quinine sulfate (Fig. S4). It is important that the as-prepared Si-CQDs exhibit up-conversion PL properties. We know that the photon avalanche, excited state absorption and energy transfer up-conversion are three basic mechanisms to explain up-conversion fluorescence. However, up-conversion PL derives from more than one fundamental mechanism in most cases. There have different reviews concerning the explanation for the up-conversion fluorescence of CQDs. Among them, the multi-photon active process, in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons, is the common explanation for up-conversion fluorescence of CQDs [1,50,51]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Si-CQDs were excited at 700 to 800 nm wavelength. The up-conversion fluorescence spectra show the fixed emission peaks at 452 nm, indicating that the fluorescence emission occurs from the lowest singlet state regardless of the excitation wavelength [52]. Unlike conventional PL imaging, the up-conversion PL not only offers lower self-fluorescence photodamage and photo-toxicity, but also allows deeper light penetration and reduced light scattering for the anti-stokes emission during excitation in the NIR range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and film-forming ability of the Si-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of the Si-CQDs, the fluorescence intensities of the Si-CQDs under different pH, temperature, ionic strengths and time illumination were further studied. The results are displayed in Fig. S5, the fluorescence behavior of the Si-CQDs is independent of the pH and the fluorescence intensity almost has no change in a wide pH range (1.81-11.82), indicating that the Si-CQDs are stable in acid and alkaline solutions (Fig. S5a). Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity is stable in ionic strength and high temperature medium. As shown in Fig. S5b and Fig. S5c, the emission intensity keeps stable even if the concentration of NaCl is high to 2 mol/L and the temperature is as high as 99 °C. In addition, the Si-CQDs also have excellent resistance to photo-bleaching. Fig. S5d shows the luminescent intensity of the Si-CQDs has no significant decrease during continuous excitation at 370 nm with Xe lamp for 1 h. The good stability indicates that the Si-CQDs have great potential in biological labeling and analytical detection.

Subsequently, the film-forming ability of the Si-CQDs has been studied. The specific operation processes are as follows: the Si-CQDs solution was coated onto the glass substrates. Then it was dried at room temperature, and thin films formed on the surface of the glass substrates, which indicated the Si-CQDs hold good film-forming properties. As shown in Fig. S6, solid-state thin films of the Si-CQDs still hold strong blue emissions under 365 nm UV light. The solid-state fluorescence renders the Si-CQDs as promising candidates for solid-state flexible optoelectronic devices.

Subsequently, the quenching mechanism was studied. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the Si-CQDs are located at 370 nm and 452 nm, respectively (Fig. 4d). While BH shows broad absorption at 228 nm, 263 nm, 350 nm and 421 nm, respectively, showing a good overlapping with the excitation and emission bands of the Si-CQDs, indicating that the inner filter effect (IFE) may be the cause of fluorescence quenching [57]. In order to further understand the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime and absorption spectrum of the Si-CQDs with BH were also measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence lifetime of the Si-CQDs in the absence and presence of 20 μmol/L BH remaines constant (from 11.25 ns to 11.21 ns) under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. And the absorption spectrums of the Si-CQDs, BH and Si-CQDs-BH is shown in Fig. 5b. Obviously, no new absorption peak but increased absorption intensity can be observed with the addition of 20 μmol/L BH. Therefore, the quenching mechanism between Si-CQDs and BH is IFE [58].

It is worth noting that Si-CQDs possess tunable fluorescence spectra. So, the excitation and emission wavelengths of the Si-CQDs are easy to control, which allows good spectral overlap between the Si-CQDs and BH, and is beneficial to improving the detection accuracy. In this work, for high sensitivity and good spectral overlap between the absorption peak of BH and the excitation and emission bands of the Si-CQDs, 350 nm is selected as the optimal excitation wavelength.

To assess the selectivity of this method, the emission intensities of the Si-CQDs solution before and after adding common metal ions, amino acid, saccharide and other coexistent substances that may present in the urine samples were also investigated. Fig. S7 shows that the emission intensity of the Si-CQDs decreased obviously in the presence of BH, whereas a negligible intensity change could be observed upon the addition of other substances. The results indicate that the proposed assay shows high sensitivity and selectivity for BH. Moreover, the effect of several coexistent substances on the detection of BH (20 μmol/L) were further studied. Given a tolerance level of ± 10%, as shown in Table S3, common amino acid, saccharide, metal ions and other coexistent substances can be allowed in high concentrations except Fe3+. However, the concentration of Fe3+ in human urine was far away lower than the interference concentration (30 μmol/L). Therefore, this sensing nanoprobe can be generalized to analyze BH in real samples based on its high selectivity and exceptional anti-interference ability.

To further study the potential applications of this sensing strategy, the determination of BH in urine samples based on standard addition method was performed. A 50 μL 100-fold diluted urinary sample of a healthy adult with various BH concentrations was added in the Si-CQDs solution, and then the fluorescence intensities were measured. Table 2 shows that the concentrations of BH obtained by this proposed strategy are well consistent with the added values and the spiked recoveries of three urinary samples were 93.7%-107.2%. The results confirmed the practicability of the proposed analysis method.

In this work, Si-CQDs with high QY (34.8%) were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal assay. The as-obtained Si-CQDs are capped with abundant Si-OH, exhibiting excellent fluorescence properties, good stability, film-forming ability, solid-state fluorescence, excellent water dispersibility and high photostability, which are convenient for surface modification and potential applications in many fields. Taking advantage of the tunable excitation and emission spectra, the Si-CQDs were applied to the sensitive determination of BH based on its down- and up-conversion fluorescence. Prospectively, the Si-CQDs will hold great potentiality for analysis and imaging in the environment, chemistry and life fields.

Jia-Man Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Fang Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yu-Lin Zhu: Formal analysis. Xiao-Yan Deng: Conceptualization, Data curation. Xian-Ping Chen: Data curation, Investigation. Qiu-Ju Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Ke-Jun Tan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21377103).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121139.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
